overview: Monstera is a tropical climbing houseplant known for its split leaves. It grows best with steady warmth, bright filtered light, and a chunky, well-draining potting mix.
careDifficultyDescription: Forgiving once light and watering are balanced, but sensitive to soggy soil.
light: Bright indirect light; avoid harsh afternoon sun.
water: Water when the top 2-3 cm of soil feels dry, usually every 7-10 days indoors.
humidityDescription: Prefers moderate to high humidity, but adapts to average indoor air.
diseaseRiskDescription: Overwatering can cause yellow leaves, root rot, and fungus gnats.
fertilizer: Feed monthly during spring and summer with a balanced diluted fertilizer.
tips: Give it a moss pole or support if you want larger, more mature leaves.

overview: Pothos is a fast-growing trailing plant with heart-shaped leaves. It is one of the easiest indoor plants for beginners and tolerates a wide range of rooms.
careDifficultyDescription: Very forgiving and quick to recover from missed watering.
light: Low to bright indirect light; brighter light keeps variegation stronger.
water: Water when the top half of the soil dries out.
humidityDescription: Handles normal home humidity well.
diseaseRiskDescription: Main risks are root rot from standing water and leggy growth in very low light.
fertilizer: Feed every 6-8 weeks in the growing season.
tips: Trim long vines to encourage fuller growth and root cuttings in water.

overview: Snake Plant has upright sword-like leaves and stores water in thick foliage. It is highly drought tolerant and works well in bedrooms, offices, and low-maintenance spaces.
careDifficultyDescription: Needs little attention and prefers being slightly neglected.
light: Low to bright indirect light; avoid sudden intense direct sun.
water: Let soil dry completely before watering, often every 2-4 weeks.
humidityDescription: Comfortable in dry indoor air.
diseaseRiskDescription: Soft leaf bases usually mean overwatering or cold damage.
fertilizer: Feed once or twice in spring and summer with diluted fertilizer.
tips: Use a pot with drainage and avoid watering into the center of the rosette.

overview: ZZ Plant has glossy upright leaves and thick rhizomes that store water. It is a reliable choice for lower-light spaces and irregular watering routines.
careDifficultyDescription: Very tolerant of missed care, but slow to show stress.
light: Low to bright indirect light; avoid strong direct sun.
water: Water only after the soil is fully dry, usually every 2-3 weeks.
humidityDescription: Average indoor humidity is fine.
diseaseRiskDescription: Yellowing stems are commonly caused by overwatering.
fertilizer: Feed lightly every 2-3 months during active growth.
tips: Rotate the pot occasionally so stems grow evenly toward the light.

overview: Peace Lily is a leafy indoor plant with white spathes that appear above dark green foliage. It prefers evenly moist soil and stable indoor conditions.
careDifficultyDescription: Easy to read because it droops when thirsty, but dislikes dry soil for too long.
light: Medium to bright indirect light; too little light reduces blooms.
water: Water when the top 2 cm of soil dries or leaves begin to soften.
humidityDescription: Appreciates moderate humidity and clean leaves.
diseaseRiskDescription: Brown tips can come from dry air, salts, or inconsistent watering.
fertilizer: Feed monthly in spring and summer at half strength.
tips: Wipe leaves regularly so the plant can photosynthesize efficiently.

overview: Spider Plant forms arching striped leaves and baby plantlets on long stems. It is adaptable, fast-growing, and simple to propagate.
careDifficultyDescription: Hardy and beginner-friendly, with visible signs when thirsty.
light: Bright indirect light for best stripes and compact growth.
water: Water when the top 2-3 cm of soil dries.
humidityDescription: Tolerates average indoor humidity.
diseaseRiskDescription: Brown tips may appear from dry soil, salts, or very hard water.
fertilizer: Feed monthly during spring and summer.
tips: Plantlets can be rooted while still attached or clipped and rooted separately.

overview: Rubber Plant has broad glossy leaves and a strong upright shape. It becomes a bold indoor tree when given bright filtered light and consistent watering.
careDifficultyDescription: Generally sturdy, but leaf drop can happen after sudden changes.
light: Bright indirect light; some gentle morning sun is helpful.
water: Water when the top 3-5 cm of soil dries.
humidityDescription: Average humidity works, but moderate humidity keeps leaves cleaner and softer.
diseaseRiskDescription: Watch for leaf drop from overwatering, drafts, or abrupt relocation.
fertilizer: Feed monthly during active growth.
tips: Dust the large leaves often and rotate the pot for even growth.

overview: Fiddle Leaf Fig is an indoor tree with large violin-shaped leaves. It rewards stable bright light and steady watering but dislikes frequent movement.
careDifficultyDescription: Beautiful but sensitive to light shifts, overwatering, and dry drafts.
light: Very bright indirect light, ideally near a large window.
water: Water deeply when the top 5 cm of soil dries, then drain fully.
humidityDescription: Prefers moderate humidity and stable air.
diseaseRiskDescription: Brown spots may indicate root stress, inconsistent watering, or low light.
fertilizer: Feed monthly in spring and summer with a foliage fertilizer.
tips: Avoid moving it often; choose a bright spot and let it settle.

overview: Chinese Money Plant has round coin-shaped leaves on slender stems. It stays compact, grows quickly in bright light, and often produces small offsets.
careDifficultyDescription: Simple to grow with bright light and careful watering.
light: Bright indirect light; rotate often for a balanced shape.
water: Water when the top 2-3 cm of soil dries.
humidityDescription: Average indoor humidity is usually enough.
diseaseRiskDescription: Drooping or cupped leaves can reflect watering stress or low light.
fertilizer: Feed monthly in spring and summer.
tips: Separate offsets once they have their own small roots.

overview: Boston Fern has soft arching fronds and a lush texture. It prefers evenly moist soil, filtered light, and higher humidity than most common houseplants.
careDifficultyDescription: Needs consistent moisture and humidity to avoid crispy fronds.
light: Medium to bright indirect light, away from harsh direct sun.
water: Keep soil lightly moist; water before it dries completely.
humidityDescription: High humidity is important for healthy fronds.
diseaseRiskDescription: Dry air and missed watering cause browning, shedding, and crispy tips.
fertilizer: Feed monthly during spring and summer with diluted fertilizer.
tips: A bright bathroom or humid corner is often better than a dry sunny shelf.

overview: Calathea is grown for broad patterned leaves with pale striping. It prefers soft filtered light, stable warmth, and consistently moist soil.
careDifficultyDescription: Sensitive to dry air, mineral-heavy water, and inconsistent watering.
light: Medium indirect light; avoid direct sun that can fade or burn leaves.
water: Keep soil evenly moist but not soggy.
humidityDescription: High humidity helps prevent curling leaves and brown edges.
diseaseRiskDescription: Brown edges often come from low humidity, dry soil, or water quality.
fertilizer: Feed lightly every 6-8 weeks in spring and summer.
tips: Use filtered or rested water if leaf edges brown despite good humidity.

overview: Heartleaf Philodendron is a trailing plant with soft heart-shaped leaves. It is adaptable, fast-growing, and works well in hanging pots or on shelves.
careDifficultyDescription: Forgiving and reliable in common indoor conditions.
light: Low to bright indirect light; brighter light makes fuller vines.
water: Water when the top third of the soil dries.
humidityDescription: Average indoor humidity is acceptable.
diseaseRiskDescription: Yellow leaves are usually linked to overwatering or low light.
fertilizer: Feed monthly during active growth.
tips: Pinch back long vines to keep the plant full.

overview: Aloe Vera is a succulent with thick water-storing leaves. It needs strong light, fast-draining soil, and long dry periods between watering.
careDifficultyDescription: Easy if treated like a succulent rather than a leafy tropical plant.
light: Bright light with several hours of gentle direct sun.
water: Water deeply, then let soil dry completely before watering again.
humidityDescription: Prefers dry air and dislikes humid, stagnant conditions.
diseaseRiskDescription: Mushy leaves and black spots usually mean excess water or poor drainage.
fertilizer: Feed once in spring with diluted succulent fertilizer.
tips: A gritty cactus mix and drainage hole are more important than frequent watering.

overview: Echeveria is a compact rosette succulent with powdery leaves. It needs strong light and dry soil to stay colorful and symmetrical.
careDifficultyDescription: Simple in strong light, but stretches quickly in dim rooms.
light: Very bright light with gentle direct sun.
water: Water only when soil is completely dry, usually every 2-3 weeks.
humidityDescription: Prefers dry air and good airflow.
diseaseRiskDescription: Overwatering and water trapped in the rosette can cause rot.
fertilizer: Feed once or twice during spring and summer with diluted succulent fertilizer.
tips: If the rosette stretches upward, it needs more light.

overview: Jade Plant is a woody succulent with thick oval leaves. It grows slowly, lives for many years, and prefers bright light with dry soil cycles.
careDifficultyDescription: Low-maintenance when kept bright and not overwatered.
light: Bright light with some direct sun.
water: Let soil dry fully before watering, often every 2-3 weeks.
humidityDescription: Dry indoor air is fine.
diseaseRiskDescription: Leaf drop or soft stems often point to excess water.
fertilizer: Feed lightly every 2-3 months during active growth.
tips: Prune lightly to keep a compact tree-like shape.

overview: Ladyfinger Cactus forms clusters of narrow cylindrical stems covered in small spines. It is compact, drought tolerant, and ideal for bright windowsills.
careDifficultyDescription: Very low care if kept dry and bright.
light: Bright direct or very bright indirect light.
water: Water sparingly only when soil is bone dry.
humidityDescription: Prefers dry air and excellent airflow.
diseaseRiskDescription: Rot is the main risk when soil stays wet.
fertilizer: Feed once in spring or early summer with cactus fertilizer.
tips: Use a gritty mineral-heavy mix and never let the pot sit in water.

overview: Moth Orchid is a common indoor orchid with long-lasting blooms. It grows in bark mix, prefers filtered light, and needs roots that can breathe.
careDifficultyDescription: Manageable once watering and root airflow are understood.
light: Bright indirect light; avoid hot direct sun.
water: Water when bark is nearly dry and roots turn silvery.
humidityDescription: Prefers moderate humidity and gentle airflow.
diseaseRiskDescription: Crown rot can occur if water sits in the center of the leaves.
fertilizer: Feed weakly every 2-4 weeks while actively growing.
tips: Keep the crown dry and let excess water drain fully after watering.

overview: Anthurium has glossy heart-shaped leaves and colorful waxy spathes. It prefers bright filtered light, airy soil, and warm humid conditions.
careDifficultyDescription: Needs moisture and humidity, but roots must still breathe.
light: Bright indirect light for steady blooms.
water: Water when the top 2-3 cm of soil dries.
humidityDescription: Higher humidity supports larger leaves and better flowers.
diseaseRiskDescription: Yellow leaves and root stress can come from dense wet soil.
fertilizer: Feed monthly at half strength during active growth.
tips: Use an airy mix with bark or perlite instead of dense potting soil.

overview: Lucky Bamboo is a Dracaena often grown in water or a light potting mix. It is compact, decorative, and tolerant of indoor light.
careDifficultyDescription: Simple if water quality and light are kept stable.
light: Low to medium indirect light; avoid direct sun.
water: If grown in water, refresh weekly; if in soil, keep lightly moist.
humidityDescription: Average indoor humidity is enough.
diseaseRiskDescription: Yellow stems can come from direct sun, poor water quality, or old water.
fertilizer: Use very diluted fertilizer every 2-3 months.
tips: Use filtered water if leaf tips brown or stems yellow.

overview: English Ivy is a trailing vine with lobed leaves. Indoors it prefers cool rooms, bright filtered light, and evenly moist soil.
careDifficultyDescription: Adaptable, but dislikes hot dry rooms and very dry soil.
light: Bright indirect light; variegated forms need more brightness.
water: Water when the top 2 cm of soil dries.
humidityDescription: Moderate humidity helps reduce crispy leaf edges.
diseaseRiskDescription: Spider mites are more likely in hot, dry indoor air.
fertilizer: Feed monthly during spring and summer.
tips: Trim vines regularly for a denser, less tangled plant.
